elements.sent-2411-001: In reconfiguration, we are given two solutions to a graph problem, such as \probVC or \probDS, with each solution represented by a placement of tokens on vertices of the graph.
elements.sent-2411-002: Our task is to reconfigure one into the other using small steps while ensuring the intermediate configurations of tokens are also valid solutions.
elements.sent-2411-003: The two commonly studied settings are \TJ and \TS, which allows moving a single token to an arbitrary or an adjacent vertex, respectively.
elements.sent-2411-004: We introduce new rules that generalize \TJ, parameterized by the number of tokens allowed to move at once and by the maximum distance of each move.
elements.sent-2411-005: Our main contribution is identifying minimal rules that allow reconfiguring any possible given solution into any other for \probIS, \probVC, and \probDS.
elements.sent-2411-006: For each minimal rule, we also provide an efficient algorithm that finds a corresponding reconfiguration sequence.
elements.sent-2411-007: We further focus on the rule that allows each token to move to an adjacent vertex in a single step.
elements.sent-2411-008: This natural variant turns out to be the minimal rule that guarantees reconfigurability for \probVC.
elements.sent-2411-009: We determine the computational complexity of deciding whether a (shortest) reconfiguration sequence exists under this rule for the three studied problems.
elements.sent-2411-010: While reachability for \probVC is shown to be in \Pclass, finding a shortest sequence is shown to be \NP-complete.
elements.sent-2411-011: For \probIS and \probDS, even reachability is shown to be \PSPACE-complete.
elements.sent-2411-012: Reconfiguration problems arise whenever we want to transform one feasible solution into another in small steps while keeping all intermediate solutions also feasible.
elements.sent-2411-013: The problem is widely studied in the context of graph problems, such as Independent Set~\cite{Lokshtanov_2019, Demaine_2015, Bonamy_2017, Belmonte_2020, Bartier_2021, Bonsma_2014, Yamada_2021, Hoang_2019 , \probVC~\cite{Mouawad_2018, ITO_2016 , Dominating Set~\cite{Bonamy_2021, Bousquet_2021, Lokshtanov_2018, Haddadan_2016, K_i_an_2023 , Shortest Paths~\cite{Kami_ski_2011, Gajjar_2022 , and Coloring~\cite{Bonsma_2009, Bonamy_2013, Bonsma_2014_2, Cereceda_2010 .}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-014: Moreover, outside of graphs, many results concern Satisfiability~\cite{Gopalan_2006, Mouawad_2017 .}
elements.sent-2411-016: In graph settings, the problem is characterized by a condition, a reconfiguration rule, an input graph, and an initial and a target configuration.
elements.sent-2411-017: Configurations (including initial and target) are characterized by tokens placed on the vertices of the graph.
elements.sent-2411-018: The condition restricts the placement of the tokens, for instance, the tokens need to form an independent set or a dominating set.
elements.sent-2411-019: The reconfiguration rule allows only certain movements of the tokens to obtain a new configuration.
elements.sent-2411-020: The goal is to move the tokens to the target configuration following the prescribed reconfiguration rule and ensuring all intermediate configurations satisfy the condition.
elements.sent-2411-021: There are two main questions about reconfiguration problems.
elements.sent-2411-022: Is the reconfiguration from the initial to the target configuration possible? And if so, what is the smallest number of steps that reconfigure the initial to the target configuration? Two reconfiguration rules are most commonly studied, Token Jumping and Token Sliding.
elements.sent-2411-023: In \TJ, only one token is allowed to be removed from the graph and then placed on another vertex.
elements.sent-2411-024: In the case of \TS, one token can be moved only to a neighboring vertex.
elements.sent-2411-025: We can understand \TS as a restriction of \TJ to moves of distance $1$.
elements.sent-2411-027: First, we examine the case where all $k$ tokens can move by distance at most $d$ and we call this rule \nkTJ{k {d .}}
elements.sent-2411-028: Second, we allow all $k$ tokens to move to distance $1$, and out of those, $k'$ tokens can move even farther to distance $d$.
elements.sent-2411-030: Note that \TS is equivalent to \nkTJ{1 {1 and on connected graphs the standard Token Jumping is equivalent to \nkTJ{1 {n .}}}}
elements.sent-2411-031: In this work, we present minimal rules that for a given condition (\probVC, \probDS, and \probIS) ensure that the reconfiguration problem is feasible for any pair of initial and target configurations in a connected graph.
elements.sent-2411-032: Conversely, we provide instances of initial and target configurations such that weaker rules do not allow reconfiguring one to the other.
elements.sent-2411-033: We further consider the complexity of the reconfiguration problems under \nkTJ{k {1 where $k$ is the size of the reconfigured solution, that is, each token can move to a neighbor.}}
elements.sent-2411-034: We show that the reachability question, i.e.\ whether a reconfiguration sequence exists, is easy for \probVC under \infTJ.
elements.sent-2411-035: On the other hand, deciding whether a reconfiguration sequence of length at most $\ell$ exists is \NP-hard, even for fixed $\ell \geq 2$.
elements.sent-2411-036: Even just the reachability question for \probDS and \probIS is shown to be \PSPACE-hard.
elements.sent-2411-037: It is known that reachability is \PSPACE-complete for \probIS under both \TJ and \TS even on graphs of bounded bandwidth~\cite{Wrochna_2018 .}
elements.sent-2411-038: The same result applies also for \probVC under \TJ and \TS if we do not allow multiple tokens on one vertex.
elements.sent-2411-039: It is also known that reachability is \PSPACE-complete for \probDS under \TS even on bipartite graphs, split graphs, and bounded bandwidth graphs~\cite{Bonamy_2021 .}
elements.sent-2411-040: The same problem is also \PSPACE-complete under \TJ~\cite{Bonamy_2021 .}
